{"parentUuid":"5e7a574c-95dd-4c72-b737-167917fb3fbd","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"a3c1f306c9b92610e","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-6","id":"msg_014m1jVJnvYcTmJVMzv7UgiV","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_01C8F7YhtJV1DnrNcgXD3Mor","name":"WebFetch","input":{"url":"https://clinicaltrials.gov/api/v2/studies/NCT01458405","prompt":"Extract all details about the ALLSTAR trial: why it was terminated, interim analysis results, design, endpoints, dates."},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":"tool_use","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":442,"cache_read_input_tokens":66602,"output_tokens":150,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":442},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"requestId":"req_011CZEmiXZ9LSucCw8nUAFHT","type":"assistant","uuid":"9fa1183a-403d-4772-acc7-cb13f2ecf0bc","timestamp":"2026-03-20T18:02:52.396Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/junekim/Documents/june.kim","sessionId":"6d5431e1-97f0-46be-9bdf-36e96721569f","version":"2.1.79","gitBranch":"master","slug":"modular-sparking-forest"}
{"parentUuid":"9fa1183a-403d-4772-acc7-cb13f2ecf0bc","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"8f8acf02-923c-41cf-91db-8847a47ae6f6","agentId":"a3c1f306c9b92610e","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01C8F7YhtJV1DnrNcgXD3Mor","type":"tool_result","content":"# ALLSTAR Trial Summary\n\n## Trial Termination\nThe trial was discontinued on **February 28, 2019**. According to the sponsor, \"Capricor ceased ongoing follow-up activities and terminated the ALLSTAR trial to focus resourcing on its active CAP-1002 program, HOPE-2.\"\n\n## Trial Design\n- **Official Title**: Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Phase I/II Study of the Safety and Efficacy of Intracoronary Delivery of Allogeneic Cardiosphere-Derived Cells\n- **Phases**: Phase 1 (safety cohort) and Phase 2 (randomized double-blind cohort)\n- **Study Period**: November 2012 to July 2017 (primary completion)\n- **Total Enrollment**: 135 participants across 30 sites\n\n## Trial Structure\n**Phase 1** (14 participants):\n- 4 received 12.5 million cells\n- 10 received 25 million cells\n\n**Phase 2** (121 participants):\n- 44 randomized to placebo\n- 77 randomized to CAP-1002 (25 million cells)\n\n## Primary Endpoints\n1. Safety events within 1 month post-infusion (myocarditis, arrhythmic death, sudden unexpected death, MACE)\n2. Percent change in myocardial infarct size at month 12\n\n## Key Results\nThe infarct size reduction showed minimal difference between groups at month 12:\n- **Placebo**: -7.11% change (SD: 12.22)\n- **CAP-1002**: -8.80% change (SD: 10.65)\n- **p-value**: 0.6453 (no significant difference)\n\nNo serious safety events were reported in either group during the 1-month post-infusion period."}]},"uuid":"703fe71b-cd03-4af4-9712-563e03186817","timestamp":"2026-03-20T18:03:00.426Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"9fa1183a-403d-4772-acc7-cb13f2ecf0bc","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/junekim/Documents/june.kim","sessionId":"6d5431e1-97f0-46be-9bdf-36e96721569f","version":"2.1.79","gitBranch":"master","slug":"modular-sparking-forest"}
{"parentUuid":"703fe71b-cd03-4af4-9712-563e03186817","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"a3c1f306c9b92610e","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-6","id":"msg_01GqddhFr5oWmBPNc78RBgvs","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I now have sufficient data to construct a comprehensive stack trace. Let me compile the final report.\n\n---\n\n# Capricor Therapeutics (CAPR) — Consolidation Stack Trace\n\n## Overview\n\nCapricor Therapeutics is the consolidation stack for Deramiocel (CAP-1002), an allogeneic cardiosphere-derived cell (CDC) therapy. The company has run four sequential programs reading outcomes and writing changes back to the compound/trial design: ALLSTAR (myocardial infarction), HOPE-1, HOPE-2, and HOPE-3 (all DMD). A BLA was filed, received a Complete Response Letter in July 2025, and was resubmitted with a new PDUFA date of August 22, 2026.\n\n---\n\n## Stage 1: READ OUTCOMES — What data did the company collect?\n\n### What I searched\n- ClinicalTrials.gov API for NCT01458405 (ALLSTAR), NCT02485938 (HOPE-1), NCT03406780 (HOPE-2), NCT05126758 (HOPE-3), NCT06304064 (HOPE-OLE)\n- PubMed for published results (PMIDs: 30674601, 35279258, 32749459)\n- SEC filings (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K) from 2018-2026\n\n### What I found\n\n**ALLSTAR (NCT01458405) — Myocardial infarction, Phase I/II, 2012-2017**\n- 135 patients, intracoronary delivery of 25M CDCs\n- Primary endpoint: % change in infarct size at 12 months\n- Result: No difference. Placebo -7.11%, CAP-1002 -8.80%, p=0.6453\n- Stopped at interim analysis for futility on primary endpoint\n- However, secondary signals emerged: LV end-diastolic volume (p=0.02), end-systolic volume (p=0.02), NT-proBNP (p=0.02) all significantly improved\n- Published: European Heart Journal, 2020 (PMID: 32749459)\n\n**HOPE-1 (NCT02485938) — DMD cardiomyopathy, Phase I/II, 2016-2017**\n- 25 patients (13 treated, 12 usual care), open-label, intracoronary delivery of 75M CDCs (25M per artery)\n- Primary endpoint: safety (events within 72 hours)\n- Secondary: cardiac MRI, PUL, 6-minute walk, pulmonary function\n- Result: Safe. Significant scar size reduction and improved inferior wall thickening. Upper limb function improved in 8/9 lower-functioning treated patients vs 0 in controls (p=0.007)\n- Published: Neurology, 2019 (PMID: 30674601)\n\n**HOPE-2 (NCT03406780) — DMD, Phase 2, 2018-2020**\n- 20 patients (8 CAP-1002, 12 placebo), double-blind, IV delivery of 150M CDCs every 3 months x 4 doses\n- Primary endpoint: change in PUL 1.2 mid-level score at 12 months\n- Result: Significant. Percentile difference 36.2 (95% CI 12.7-59.7), 2.6-point difference, p=0.014. Cardiac measures also improved.\n- Safety: 3 hypersensitivity reactions (1 severe leading to discontinuation)\n- Published: Lancet, 2022 (PMID: 35279258)\n\n**HOPE-2 OLE — Long-term follow-up**\n- 4-year data showed continued preservation of cardiac function (LVEF) and 52% slowing of skeletal muscle decline\n- 8 patients from original HOPE-1 usual-care arm crossed over to receive IV CDCs\n\n**HOPE-3 (NCT05126758) — DMD, Phase 3, 2022-present**\n- 106 patients (two cohorts), double-blind, placebo-controlled, IV delivery of 150M CDCs every 3 months x 4 doses\n- Primary endpoint: mean % change in PUL v2.0 total score at 12 months\n- Key secondary: LVEF change by cardiac MRI\n- Result (Dec 2025 topline): Met primary endpoint (PUL v2.0, p=0.03) and key secondary (LVEF, p=0.04)\n\n### Assessment: READ stage appears FUNCTIONAL\n\nThe company collected the right data at each stage. Endpoints progressed logically: safety first (HOPE-1), then functional efficacy with PUL 1.2 (HOPE-2), then confirmatory with PUL v2.0 and cardiac secondary (HOPE-3). They tracked both skeletal muscle function and cardiac parameters across all DMD trials, which caught the dual-benefit signal that ALLSTAR's cardiac-only endpoints missed.\n\n### What I ruled out\n- No evidence the company cherry-picked or changed primary endpoints post-hoc within a trial (HOPE-2 primary was pre-specified as PUL mid-level)\n- HOPE-3 did have a protocol amendment to designate LVEF as the primary efficacy endpoint at one point, per the 10-Q, though PUL v2.0 total score is listed as primary on ClinicalTrials.gov\n\n---\n\n## Stage 2: BATCH PROCESS — How did the company interpret results and decide what to change?\n\n### What I searched\n- SEC 10-K filings (2018-2026) for design rationale\n- ClinicalTrials.gov protocol comparisons\n- Published trial papers\n\n### What I found\n\n**ALLSTAR to HOPE-1 transition (2012-2016): Indication pivot**\n- ALLSTAR failed its primary endpoint (scar reduction) in MI patients, but secondary cardiac remodeling signals (volume reduction, biomarker improvement) were real\n- The company abandoned MI entirely in 2017, forgiving a $15.7M CIRM loan\n- Simultaneously, HOPE-1 was already running in DMD (started Jan 2016), meaning the pivot was not purely reactive — they had a parallel bet running\n- Interpretation: CDCs don't shrink scars but may protect function. DMD patients have ongoing progressive damage where functional preservation matters more than scar regression. This is a genuine conceptual reframe, not just shopping for a friendlier indication\n\n**HOPE-1 to HOPE-2 transition (2017-2018): Route and dose change**\n- HOPE-1: intracoronary, 75M cells, single dose, open-label\n- HOPE-2: IV, 150M cells, repeated dosing (4x over 12 months), double-blind placebo-controlled\n- The shift from intracoronary to IV is structurally significant. Intracoronary delivery requires cardiac catheterization — invasive, limits repeat dosing, restricts to patients with adequate coronary access. IV allows repeat dosing in a clinic setting\n- The dose doubled (75M to 150M) and became systemic rather than local\n- The shift to blinded, placebo-controlled design was the right methodological correction from open-label HOPE-1\n- No explicit published rationale for the route change was found in the documents I accessed, but the functional logic (repeat dosing for progressive disease requires non-invasive route) is coherent\n\n**HOPE-2 to HOPE-3 transition (2020-2022): Scale-up and endpoint refinement**\n- N increased from 20 to 106\n- Endpoint upgraded from PUL 1.2 mid-level to PUL v2.0 total score (the newer, validated instrument)\n- LVEF added as key secondary endpoint — directly incorporating the cardiac signal from HOPE-1 and HOPE-2\n- Same dosing regimen (150M IV every 3 months x 4 doses) maintained\n- Two cohorts (ambulatory and non-ambulatory) rather than mixed\n\n### Assessment: BATCH PROCESS appears FUNCTIONAL with one notable concern\n\nEvidence of genuine learning:\n1. They correctly read ALLSTAR's failure — the mechanism doesn't regenerate damaged tissue but may protect remaining function\n2. They changed the route of administration to enable the repeat-dosing needed for a progressive disease\n3. They upgraded the endpoint instrument and added cardiac secondary to capture both axes of benefit\n4. They escalated from open-label to double-blind to Phase 3\n\nThe concern: The HOPE-3 protocol amendment to make LVEF the primary endpoint (mentioned in the 10-Q) vs. the ClinicalTrials.gov listing of PUL v2.0 as primary suggests possible endpoint uncertainty during the trial. This could indicate either adaptive learning (incorporating FDA feedback) or post-hoc optimization. The fact that both endpoints ultimately hit (p=0.03 and p=0.04 respectively) makes this less worrying in retrospect, but the p-values are not emphatic.\n\n---\n\n## Stage 3: WRITE SUBSTRATE — What actually changed in the molecule, manufacturing, trial design, or approach?\n\n### What I searched\n- ClinicalTrials.gov protocol details for all trials\n- SEC filings for manufacturing and BLA details\n- Endpoints News article index for CRL, Form 483, adcom\n\n### What I found\n\n**Structural changes (not cosmetic):**\n\n| Dimension | ALLSTAR | HOPE-1 | HOPE-2 | HOPE-3 |\n|-----------|---------|--------|--------|--------|\n| Indication | MI | DMD | DMD | DMD |\n| Route | Intracoronary | Intracoronary | IV | IV |\n| Dose | 25M cells | 75M cells | 150M cells | 150M cells |\n| Dosing frequency | Single | Single | 4x q3mo | 4x q3mo |\n| Blinding | Double-blind | Open-label | Double-blind | Double-blind |\n| N | 135 | 25 | 20 | 106 |\n| Primary endpoint | Scar size reduction | Safety | PUL 1.2 mid-level | PUL v2.0 total |\n| Phase | I/II | I/II | 2 | 3 |\n\n**Manufacturing:**\n- New San Diego GMP facility built and operational (2023-2024)\n- Pre-License Inspection (PLI) completed — FDA issued Form 483 observations\n- All 483 observations subsequently resolved and accepted by FDA\n- Facility described as ready for commercial launch\n\n**Regulatory pathway:**\n- RMAT designation obtained (date not precisely found in documents, but confirmed in 2018 10-K and subsequent filings)\n- Rare Pediatric Disease designation\n- Orphan Drug designation (referenced but date not found)\n- BLA submitted with Priority Review; original PDUFA: August 31, 2025\n- CRL received July 11, 2025\n- Type A meeting August 2025 — FDA supported submission of HOPE-3 results to address CRL issues\n- BLA resubmitted as Class 2 resubmission; new PDUFA: August 22, 2026\n- Nippon Shinyaku partnership: $30M upfront + up to $705M milestones (US and Japan rights), expanded to Japan in 2023 for additional $12M + $89M milestones\n\n**The CRL — what went wrong:**\n- The specific deficiencies in the CRL were not publicly disclosed in detail\n- What is known: Form 483 observations were issued during the PLI (manufacturing inspection) and subsequently resolved\n- The FDA \"supported the submission of the HOPE-3 results in order to address the issues raised in the CRL\" (per Nov 2025 10-Q), indicating the CRL's issues were at least partly about the clinical evidence package, not solely manufacturing\n- The company's strategy was to resubmit based on existing data + HOPE-3 results as \"supportive and confirmatory evidence\"\n- An FDA advisory committee was scheduled to review the application (per Endpoints News index), though no voting outcome was found\n- An Endpoints News headline from August 2025 mentions \"With Prasad Back at FDA\" in the context of Capricor fighting the rejection, suggesting the CRL may have been influenced by the leadership turmoil at FDA/CBER during 2025\n\n### Assessment: WRITE SUBSTRATE appears FUNCTIONAL — changes were structural, not superficial\n\nThe changes between trials were substantive:\n1. **Indication change** (MI to DMD) — not a tweak, a fundamental reframe of which patients benefit\n2. **Route change** (intracoronary to IV) — enabled the repeat-dosing model essential for progressive disease\n3. **Dose escalation** (25M to 150M) — 6x increase\n4. **Design rigor** (open-label to double-blind placebo-controlled) — methodological upgrade\n5. **Manufacturing** (new facility, PLI completed, 483s resolved) — commercial-readiness investment\n6. **Endpoint evolution** (scar size to PUL to PUL v2.0 + LVEF) — aligned with disease biology\n\n---\n\n## Trauma Recurrence Analysis\n\n**Prior failure: ALLSTAR (MI) — stopped for futility on primary endpoint (scar reduction)**\n\n**Did the same class of failure recur?**\n\nThe ALLSTAR failure class was: CDCs delivered intracoronary don't regenerate scarred tissue. The company's response was threefold:\n1. Change the disease context (DMD — where preservation matters more than regeneration)\n2. Change the route (IV — enabling repeat systemic dosing)\n3. Change the endpoint (functional preservation rather than structural repair)\n\nThe CRL in July 2025 represents a second setback, but it appears to be a *different class* of failure — likely a combination of manufacturing observations (Form 483) and insufficient clinical evidence package (FDA wanted HOPE-3 data). This is a regulatory packaging failure, not a mechanism-of-action failure. The company responded by resolving the 483s and submitting HOPE-3 data per FDA guidance from the Type A meeting. The new PDUFA is August 22, 2026.\n\n**Evidence the same failure did NOT recur:** They never went back to scar reduction endpoints, never returned to intracoronary delivery, never re-attempted the MI indication. The ALLSTAR lesson was absorbed.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Verdict\n\n| Stage | Status | Evidence |\n|-------|--------|----------|\n| **Read Outcomes** | Functional | Collected right data across all 4 programs; tracked both cardiac and skeletal endpoints in DMD |\n| **Batch Process** | Functional | Correctly interpreted ALLSTAR failure as wrong-indication-and-route; genuine conceptual reframe to DMD + IV + repeat dosing |\n| **Write Substrate** | Functional | Structural changes across trials (indication, route, dose, endpoints, manufacturing); not cosmetic adjustments |\n| **Trauma recurrence** | No recurrence | ALLSTAR (mechanism/indication failure) did not recur; CRL (regulatory/manufacturing/evidence packaging) is a different failure class |\n\n**Key uncertainty:** The CRL's specific deficiencies remain undisclosed. The Form 483 was resolved, and FDA accepted HOPE-3 data as responsive, but we lack the actual CRL letter to know whether FDA had efficacy concerns (the p-values of 0.03 and 0.04 in HOPE-3 are real but narrow). The advisory committee meeting that was scheduled would have been the most revealing document — it would show what FDA's reviewers actually thought about the evidence — but I could not access the briefing documents or voting record.\n\n---\n\n## Sources\n\n- **ClinicalTrials.gov API:** NCT01458405 (ALLSTAR), NCT02485938 (HOPE-1), NCT03406780 (HOPE-2), NCT05126758 (HOPE-3), NCT06304064 (HOPE-OLE)\n- **PubMed:** PMID 32749459 (ALLSTAR, Eur Heart J 2020), PMID 30674601 (HOPE-1, Neurology 2019), PMID 35279258 (HOPE-2, Lancet 2022)\n- **SEC EDGAR:** 8-K filings dated 2025-07-11 (CRL), 2025-08-11 (Q2 results + 483 resolution), 2025-11-10 (Q3 results + Type A outcome), 2025-12-03 (HOPE-3 topline), 2026-03-10 (new PDUFA), 2026-03-17 (10-K annual report); accession numbers confirmed via EDGAR full-text search API\n- **Capricor website:** capricor.com (pipeline, homepage with PDUFA announcement)\n- **Endpoints News index:** Articles confirming CRL (July 2025), Form 483, adcom scheduled, HOPE-3 success (Dec 2025)\n- **Biospace index:** Articles confirming Type A meeting (Aug 2025), CRL response (Sep 2025), BLA resubmission (Dec 2025), new PDUFA (Mar 2026)\n- **StockAnalysis.com:** PDUFA date August 22, 2026; Class 2 resubmission confirmed"}],"stop_reason":"end_turn","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":602,"cache_read_input_tokens":67044,"output_tokens":4169,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":602},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"requestId":"req_011CZEmjaCRQv7cksLS8uTyQ","type":"assistant","uuid":"3b65828f-494b-48c3-a9c8-89014334e727","timestamp":"2026-03-20T18:04:32.493Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/junekim/Documents/june.kim","sessionId":"6d5431e1-97f0-46be-9bdf-36e96721569f","version":"2.1.79","gitBranch":"master","slug":"modular-sparking-forest"}
